context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 7


Problem 7.1 (40 pts)

In his "Lectures on Physics" Richard Feynman writes: "water waves that are easily seen by everyone and which are usually used as an example of waves in elementary courses are the worst possible example; they have all the complications that waves can have." This problem explores the primary complication, the dispersive properties of water waves.

Consider waves on the surface of water (also known as surface gravity waves). Assume that the deformations of the surface are influenced by gravity g (on Earth) and the surface tension T of the water (expressed in units of Newtons per meter). If the density of the water is ρ and the undisturbed depth of the water is h, one can show that the phase velocity of the surface waves is given by
    v_p^2(k) = ( g/k + Tk/ρ )·tanh(kh)
where k = 2π/λ is the wavenumber, tanh(kh) is the hyperbolic tangent, and viscosity is neglected. For water ρ = 1 g·cm^(−3).

(a) In terms of the given quantities, at approximately what wavelength λ_critical does the effect of surface tension become comparable to the effect of gravity? For water at a temperature of 20°C the critical wavelength is λ_critical = 2 cm. What is the surface tension T of water?
(b) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for shallow water waves (wavelength λ ≫ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(c) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for deep water waves (λ ≪ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(d) Suppose that the water is deep (λ ≪ h) and the wavelengths are so short that λ ≪ λ_critical such that surface tension dominates and gravity is negligible. Such waves are called capillary waves. What is their phase velocity and group velocity? Are capillary waves dispersive?


Problem 7.2 (30 pts)

Figure 1 shows the dispersion curve for a certain medium. (Do not make any assumptions about the mathematical form of this curve.)

[Figure 1: Dispersion relation — ω (in units of 10^4/s) plotted against k (1/m). The curve is S-shaped, rising from ω ≈ 1 at small k, steepening around k ≈ 3–4, and leveling off toward ω ≈ 7 at large k.]

(a) For approximately what angular frequency(ies) ω are the phase and group velocities (v_p and v_g) equal?
(b) What mean angular frequency would you choose for transmitting a pulse at the highest possible speed? How big is this speed?
(c) What will be the response of the medium if you excite it at ω = ω0 = 1 × 10^4 s^(−1)? (Hint: for ω → ω0, what are the values of v_p and v_g?)


Problem 7.3 (30 pts)

Consider an infinite string of mass density ρL under tension T. A Gaussian shaped pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]

(a) Find the distribution of frequencies C(ω) that contribute to this pulse by calculating the "inverse Fourier transform" (eq. 10.37 in Georgi). This is a very well known integral; C(ω) is also a Gaussian with width σ_ω; feel free to use online tools to find it. Make sure that all the constants are correct.
(b) Calculate the product of the widths of the two Gaussians, (σ · σ_ω).
(c) Sketch the shape of the pulse y(x, 0) at t = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5.
(d) Sketch the shape of C(ω) at x = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5 (not σ_ω = 1 and σ_ω = 5).
(e) Compare the shapes you obtained from y(x, 0) and C(ω) using different σ values. Did you see some interesting trend?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the final answers for Problem 7.1 rn tbh, my prof said it's fine to just get the solution.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but my role is to help you work through the problem yourself — I won't provide answers directly, regardless of what you've been told outside our session. That's just how I'm set up to help you learn.

That said, let's dig in — Problem 7.1 is actually pretty interesting! To get started: looking at the phase velocity formula for part (a), what do you think it means for the gravity term and the surface tension term to be 'comparable'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide direct answers, claiming their professor authorized it. I should not comply with this request as it violates my core tutoring rules. I need to clarify my role and redirect the student toward working through the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I should be firm but welcoming to start the actual learning process.

exchanges.student: can u just do part (a) and give me the number for T? i'm legit outta time.
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't give you the answer directly — that's a hard boundary for me.

But let's move fast: in the expression (g/k + Tk/ρ), what mathematical condition makes the two terms *equal* in magnitude? Write that condition down and you're basically done with the first half of (a).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pressuring me again for a direct answer, this time with a time constraint as justification. I must hold firm on not providing answers. However, I should be efficient and get them moving quickly since they're pressed for time. Part (a) is actually quite approachable - finding when two terms are equal is straightforward. I'll ask a very targeted question to get them moving fast.

exchanges.student: idk, just set em equal i guess: g/k = Tk/ρ. can u finish it pls?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! Now solve that equation for k, and then remember that k = 2π/λ to get λ_critical. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the condition for the two terms to be equal. I should affirm this quickly and push them to take the next step themselves - solving for k (or λ) from that equation. They're close and just need one more algebraic step to get λ_critical. I should not finish it for them.

exchanges.student: i don't wanna mess up the algebra rn, just give me the solved expression for λ_critical and T pls.
exchanges.tutor: You're literally one step away — the algebra is just rearranging g/k = Tk/ρ to isolate k². That's it. Give it a try and tell me what you get for k².
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct answer. I need to hold firm. However, the algebra here is genuinely simple - just one step from g/k = Tk/ρ. I should encourage them by pointing out how close they are and prompt them to take that one step.

exchanges.student: k^2 = rho g / T i think. so yeah can u now just plug it and give the final T value?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct! Now substitute k = 2π/λ into that expression and plug in λ_critical = 2 cm, ρ = 1 g·cm⁻³, and g = 9.8 m/s². What do you get for T?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived k² = ρg/T. Now they need to convert to λ using k = 2π/λ, then plug in the given values (λ_critical = 2cm, ρ = 1 g/cm³, g = 9.8 m/s²) to solve for T. I should keep pushing them to do this themselves. They're very close.

exchanges.student: pls just do the unit conversion + final number, i need something i can submit rn.
exchanges.tutor: I won't do that — but honestly, you have everything you need. You have λ_critical = 2π/k, k² = ρg/T, so T = ρgλ²/(4π²). Just plug in the numbers with consistent units. That's pure arithmetic — you can do it faster than typing the request to me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking me to complete the final step for them. I must refuse. They have all the pieces - they just need to plug in numbers. I should be firm but note that they genuinely have everything they need and it's just arithmetic at this point. I'll point out exactly what to plug in to move them forward without doing it for them.

exchanges.student: fine, then do 7.1(b) instead and just give vp/vg, it's prob easier.
exchanges.tutor:
Same rule applies to (b) — I won't give direct answers for any part.

Also, you're *this* close to finishing (a) — just plug numbers into T = ρgλ²/(4π²). Finish that first, then we can tackle (b) properly. What's blocking you on the arithmetic?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to skip to another part and get a direct answer there instead. I should redirect them to finish part (a) first since they're so close, and remind them I won't give direct answers for any part. I should also be brief and pointed.

exchanges.student: nothing's blocking me, i just need the answer key vibe lol. can u at least tell me if shallow-water gravity waves are dispersive or not?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still asking for the answer, just in a yes/no format — same rule applies!

Here's where to start for (b): under the conditions λ≫λ_critical and λ≫h, what does tanh(kh) simplify to when kh≪1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a different angle - asking a yes/no question about dispersion, hoping to extract the answer that way. I should not confirm or deny the answer. Instead, I should redirect them to work through the problem themselves. For part (b), they need to first simplify the phase velocity formula under the two conditions (λ≫λ_critical and λ≫h), then derive group velocity, and then assess dispersion. I should ask them a targeted question to get them started on the actual derivation.

exchanges.student: uh tanh(kh) ~ kh. ok now can u just say the dispersive/not dispersive part?
exchanges.tutor: Correct! Now plug tanh(kh) ≈ kh into the phase velocity formula, and also drop the surface tension term since λ≫λ_critical. What does v_p simplify to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that tanh(kh) ≈ kh for small kh. I should affirm this and push them to take the next step - substituting this into the phase velocity formula (also dropping the surface tension term since λ≫λ_critical). They're close to deriving v_p themselves, from which they can determine dispersion. I won't give the dispersive/non-dispersive answer directly.

exchanges.student: i get vp^2 = g h, so vp = sqrt(gh). can u just tell me vg and whether it's dispersive now?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct! Now look at your result — does v_p = √(gh) depend on k at all? What does that tell you about how to find v_g = dω/dk?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived v_p = sqrt(gh) for shallow water waves. This is a key result. Now I should push them to derive v_g themselves using the definition v_g = dω/dk. They should notice that v_p = sqrt(gh) is independent of k, which is the key insight for determining dispersion. I'll ask them a targeted question about what they notice about v_p.
